data.title: Palo Alto Networks continues to expand margins despite M&A integrations: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) appears primed to continue improving its margins despite dealing with the acquisition integrations of CyberArk and Chronosphere, according to Morgan Stanley.

"Management was clear that recent gross margin variability is primarily a function of mix rather than execution issues or anything structural," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Meta Marshall, in a Tuesday investor note. "Product gross margins continue to be impacted by a higher mix of lower-margin hardware appliances as well as component cost impacts."

"On the services side, gross margins in the latest quarter were slightly lower QoQ due to increasing mix of high-growth SaaS offerings, which should improve going forward with scaling efficiencies," she added. "Importantly, management reinforced its long-standing focus on profitability, particularly in the context of M&A. The company remains disciplined in ensuring acquisitions scale to Palo Alto's margin profile over time, which underpins confidence in the path to 40%+ FCF margins longer term."

Morgan Stanley rates the stock at Overweight with a $223 price target.

The investment firm also noted Palo Alto's traction across AI security, SIEM, SASE, and cloud security, as well as continued progress with its platformization strategy.

"Palo Alto Networks sees a long runway for platformizations, which remain a core growth driver given the 'single pane of glass' value proposition and resonates well with customers looking to consolidate an increasingly fragmented security stack," Marshall said. 

"Importantly, Palo Alto Networks is seeing improved sales execution across the portfolio, with more reps ramped to sell multi-product solutions (aided in part by AI), supporting broader platform adoption over time," she added.

Last week, Benchmark initiated coverage on Palo Alto with a Buy rating, citing evidence that its Next Gen Security annual recurring revenue is expanding at a "tremendous scale."

data.title: Ethereum stablecoin supply hits $180B ATH: Is ETH demand mispriced?
data.description:
What if Ethereum (ETH-USD)'s real growth story doesn't lie in price but in the money quietly flowing through it?

According to Token Terminal, stablecoin supply on Ethereum (ETH-USD) has hit an ATH of ~$180B, up ~150% in 3 years. With a 60% market share, the asset isn’t just leading but rather becoming the financial highway for digital dollars.

The bigger picture? Reportedly, up to ~$1.7T could move on-chain in four years. Even if ETH's share drops to 50%, that still implies ~$850B in potential inflows. Stablecoins tell the clearest story here. 

Tether (USDT-USD) leads with ~$94B, while USD Coin (USDC) follows at ~$55B. But what’s interesting is the rise of newer players—USDS, USDe, and PYUSD—slowly gaining ground and making the stablecoin space more competitive.

The bullish ETH momentum doesn't end here. Data from Artemis shows the asset added ~$9.4B in stablecoins in just one month, while Solana (SOL-USD) lost around ~$820M, hinting that the big-whale money is clearly moving toward the biggest altcoin right now.

Institutional flows are also aligning with this trend. Data from SoSoValue shows Bitcoin (BTC-USD) ETFs pulled ~$471M in inflows on April 6, led by BlackRock's (IBIT), while Ethereum (ETH-USD) ETFs recorded ~$120M in inflows, led by BlackRock’s (ETHA) at ~$60.82M, with zero outflows across top funds.

However, investors should note that in the short term, things are a bit shaky. The ETH price is hovering around ~$2.06K, down ~4% in a day, as global tensions pulled down risk assets like BTC and even dragged down some major crypto-linked stocks such as MicroStrategy (MSTR), Robinhood (HOOD), and Mara Holdings (MARA) to about 2%-6%.

But zoom out a bit, and the picture looks more promising with stablecoin flows steadily rising on Ethereum (ETH-USD). Analyst Ali Martinez recently shared that ~$1.8K is the key level to watch—if ETH holds above this, it could possibly open the path toward ~$4.9K.

data.title: Another deadline, another pause? Trump’s Iran clock may tick longer
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz could change if talks with the Middle Eastern country progress on Tuesday. 

Multiple reports said the U.S. is preparing to carry out "heavy strikes" against the country if the deadline is not met, but if talks progress, that can change. 

However, if the deadline ends without any agreement, then the U.S. is looking to launch an attack. Iran has also reportedly ended diplomatic talks with Washington. 

The news comes hours after Trump issued a warning where he threatened the destruction of “a whole civilization” if Tehran does not agree to U.S. terms to end the war in the area.

The president also gave a deadline of 8 p.m. ET for Iran to accept U.S. conditions and open the Strait of Hormuz, adding that all of Iran could be "taken out in one night" if the deadline isn't met.

On the other hand, reports surfaced that the U.S. has attacked military targets on Kharg Island.

Despite Iran allegedly cutting off communication with the U.S., Middle Eastern officials said talks with ceasefire mediators continue, although efforts are now complicated.

If Trump extended the deadline, this would be the fourth time to do so. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: BP Chair Manifold faces re-election battle as opposition mounts over blocked climate resolution
data.description:
Proxy advisor Glass Lewis and investment group Legal & General are recommending shareholders vote against Albert Manifold's re-election as BP's (BP) Chair over the company's decision to block a climate-related resolution from its April 23 annual general meeting, the Financial Times reported Tuesday.

Glass Lewis said Manifold was ultimately accountable for BP's (BP) decision to exclude a resolution filed by climate activist group Follow This that called on the company to disclose how its strategy would perform under scenarios of declining demand for oil and gas.

The exclusion of the proposal had not been necessary and raised "concerns about transparency, shareholder communication and responsiveness to shareholder concerns," Glass Lewis wrote.

Glass Lewis joined ISS, another influential advisor, in recommending shareholders reject BP's (BP) proposal that it no longer needed to make certain climate-related disclosures.

Legal & General said the exclusion amounted to a "reduction in transparency" that would make it more difficult for shareholders to understand how BP (BP) was addressing the risks related to the energy transition.

The growing controversy is a test for Meg O'Neill, who joined BP (BP) as CEO this month.

data.title: Basic necessities stocks are underperforming the S&P 500 for the last three years
data.description:
A group of four basic supermarket staples companies has been underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) for the last three years to levels not seen since March 2000.

“We are at a year 2000 performance extreme on basic supermarket staples companies relative to the S&P 500 (SP500),” said Jeff Weniger, WisdomTree head of equities.

 

 

 

The three-year annualized performance differential of The Campbell's Co. (CPB), General Mills (GIS), The Clorox Co. (CLX), and Conagra Brands (CAG)—compared to the S&P 500 (SP500)—is at -39.1 percentage points in 2026. It was down 42.7 percentage points in 2024.

“Many consumer staples and food-related stocks are priced as if pressure on the consumer will persist indefinitely,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Muhammad Ibrahim Ihsan in a recent analysis.

“But the consumer doesn’t need to get stronger for these stocks to work—they just need to stop deteriorating. With policy support, modest event-driven demand, easing cost pressure, and valuations already reflecting pessimism, 2026 could turn out better than the market currently expects,” he added.

data.title: Datadog, Snowflake seen 'accelerating' growth as AI spending continues: BofA
data.description:
Datadog (DDOG) and Snowflake (SNOW) are likely to keep “accelerating” their respective growth rates as artificial intelligence-related spending continues, Bank of America said.

“Datadog total revenue grew 29.2% y/y versus +28.4% last quarter, with broad‑based sequential usage acceleration across customer cohorts,” analyst Koji Iheda wrote in a note to clients. “Snowflake Product revenue grew +30.0% versus 28.7% last quarter, driven by strength in the core business and AI tools like Intelligence and Cortex AI.”

Iheda continued: “With AI still not driving meaningful growth for the vast majority of software vendors, we are finding that investors are favoring stocks where growth is accelerating with its core offerings. We think Datadog was one of the standouts in 4Q25, where total revenue growth excluding AI-natives accelerated to +23% y/y, up 3 points from +20% in 3Q25. Moreover, the 3-point acceleration was better than the 2-point acceleration delivered in 3Q25. From an AI growth contribution perspective, we think Snowflake’s commentary on Intelligence (used in 2,500 accounts up 2x q/q), Cortex AI, and Cortex Code was bullish, as was Elastic calling out +6 pts faster growth among customers who use AI products.”

Datadog and Snowflake shares fell in late morning trading on Tuesday.

data.title: Iran conflict fuels fertilizer rally, setting stage for stronger H2: RBC
data.description:
Fertilizer markets got an unexpected jolt in early 2026, as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East rippled through global supply chains, lifting prices and reshaping earnings expectations for major producers, according to an April 7 research report from RBC Capital Markets.

While the immediate financial impact on first-quarter results is expected to be modest, analysts led by Andrew Wong say the real earnings upside will likely emerge in the months ahead.

War-driven supply shock lifts prices

The conflict involving Iran has disrupted exports from a key fertilizer-producing region, tightening global supply, particularly for nitrogen-based products. At the same time, higher liquefied natural gas prices have pushed up production costs worldwide, reinforcing the upward pressure on prices.

Nitrogen markets have seen the most pronounced impact, with prices rising sharply. Phosphate prices have also increased, though gains have been partially offset by higher input costs such as sulphur and ammonia. Potash, by contrast, has remained relatively stable amid balanced supply and demand.

Despite the surge, many producers had already locked in sales volumes before the conflict escalated in early March, limiting the benefit to first-quarter earnings.

Earnings upside shifts to Q2 and beyond

RBC analysts expect the stronger pricing environment to meaningfully boost financial performance beginning in the second quarter, as new contracts reflect higher market prices.

The outlook remains highly dependent on the duration of the conflict. A prolonged disruption, particularly if shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains constrained, could sustain elevated fertilizer prices, while a resolution could trigger normalization later in the year.

Nitrogen producers stand out

Companies with heavy exposure to nitrogen are positioned to benefit the most from the current pricing environment. However, RBC cautions that much of this upside may already be reflected in share prices.

CF Industries (CF) and LSB Industries (LXU) are both expected to generate strong cash flows from higher nitrogen prices, but analysts see their valuations as largely pricing in these gains.

In contrast, Nutrien (NTR) is viewed as offering a more attractive risk-reward profile. Its shares have lagged the recent fertilizer rally, despite solid operations and exposure to improving nitrogen markets, leaving room for potential re-rating.

Mosaic (MOS), meanwhile, is navigating what analysts describe as a transition year. High input costs are expected to pressure phosphate margins through mid-2026, though operational improvements could position the company for a stronger recovery in 2027.

Tight supply, uncertain demand

Looking ahead, RBC forecasts continued tightness in nitrogen and phosphate markets in the near term, with prices remaining elevated before gradually moderating as global energy supply improves later in the decade.

However, affordability remains a key risk. Higher fertilizer prices, combined with elevated input costs for farmers, could dampen demand if crop prices fail to keep pace.

For now, the fertilizer sector finds itself at the intersection of geopolitics and agriculture, where the trajectory of a distant conflict may determine the next leg of earnings growth.

data.title: Overseas refiners paying record high prices ‌for some crude oil grades, far exceeding futures
data.description:
European and Asian refiners are paying record high prices ‌of near $150/bbl for some physical crude oil grades, far exceeding prices in the futures markets, highlighting the tightening supply from the Middle East war due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Competition for supply from Asian ​and European refiners to replace disrupted Middle East oil flows has helped to drive up the prices of replacement crudes for more immediate delivery, lifting some crudes to ​records; the outright price of North Sea Forties crude reached an all-time high of $146.09/bbl on Tuesday, with the "panic" over supplies driving physical prices higher, Reuters reported.

The price of Forties and many other cargoes are linked to the ​dated Brent physical crude benchmark, which ⁠is trading nearly $20/bbl higher than the price of Brent futures for June delivery because it reflects the price of cargoes ​for immediate delivery.

"The market is scrambling for prompt, refinery-usable barrels, ​and stress ⁠is appearing first in the part of the benchmark that is closest to the immediate physical problem," Morgan Stanley analysts said in a report.

"It does not mean the futures market is broken. It just means that different parts of the complex are pricing different combinations of immediacy, tightness and expected persistence," the report added.

Prices of refined products in Europe are near record highs, according to Reuters, with jet fuel prices at $226.40/bbl and diesel prices at $203.59/bbl, though still below record highs from 2022.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI)

data.title: Equinix cut to Sector Perform at Scotiabank as stock gets ahead of earnings
data.description:
Equinix (EQIX) stock dipped 1.6% in late morning trading on Tuesday after Scotiabank downgraded the data center REIT stock to Sector Perform from Sector Outperform. The cut comes after the stock significantly outperformed the S&P 500 year-to-date on the expectation of rising demand fueled by AI growth.

The stock's 23x EBITDA and 24x AFFO multiples now leave little upside potential in the near term, barring a meaningful guidance boost in the upcoming report period, the analyst said.

"While we continue to believe that Equinix’s outlook remains strong and expect earnings momentum to improve in the back half of the year, the stock’s recent performance and valuations have caused us to move to a more neutral position until earnings can catch up," Scotiabank analyst Maher Yaghi wrote in a note to clients.

Scotia raised its price target on Equinix (EQIX) to $1,050 from $997.

The Sector Perform rating aligns with the SA Quant rating and average SA Analyst rating of Hold and diverges from the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

data.title: Italy weighs leadership change at Leonardo as shares slide
data.description:
Shares of Leonardo SpA (FINMF) (FINMY) dropped sharply Tuesday amid reports that the Italian government is preparing to replace Chief Executive Officer Roberto Cingolani, who has overseen a recent turnaround at the defense group.

The stock declined as much as 7.9% during trading in Milan, marking its steepest intraday fall since August, before paring losses slightly to trade down about 7.2% by mid-afternoon.

According to a report by Bloomberg News, a successor could be named within days. Potential candidates include Alessandro Ercolani of Rheinmetall Italia (RNMBF) (RNMBY) and Lorenzo Mariani of MBDA Missile Systems Services SAS, Bloomberg News reported, citing sources familiar with the matter.

The possible leadership shake-up comes as Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni negotiates appointments across several state-backed companies with coalition partners, including Antonio Tajani of Forza Italia and Matteo Salvini of League. Momentum for changes appears to have increased following a failed referendum that weakened Meloni’s political standing.

Earlier reporting indicated that leadership continuity at Leonardo had been expected ahead of elections scheduled by 2027, but recent developments suggest a shift in direction.

Analysts say that while Cingolani’s exit would remove a leader who consistently exceeded financial expectations, it is unlikely to materially alter the company’s broader strategic trajectory or industry positioning.

Appointed in 2023, the 64-year-old former government minister had been viewed as relatively secure in his role. During his tenure, Leonardo pursued partnerships with Rheinmetall AG (RNMBF) (RNMBY) on tank production and with Baykar Technologies on unmanned systems. The company also advanced a satellite collaboration with Airbus SE and Thales SA, while divesting select operations and developing a European air-defense platform.

Leonardo reported revenue of €19.5 billion in 2025, representing a 28% increase compared with 2023.

Italian newspaper La Repubblica reported over the weekend that Cingolani was not included in the government’s proposed list of board nominees, further signaling a potential leadership transition.
